english_translation: I bow to Shiva — the auspicious one, the pure consciousness within all.
context_note: The Panchakshara mantra — five sacred syllables representing the five elements. The most widely chanted Shaivite mantra.
go_deeper_teaser: Why Om Namah Shivaya changes your nervous system →

english_translation: We worship the three-eyed one who nourishes and liberates us from the bondage of death.
context_note: The Maha Mrityunjaya mantra from the Rigveda. Chanted for healing, longevity, and liberation from fear.
go_deeper_teaser: The science of the Maha Mrityunjaya mantra →

original_script: ॐ नमः शिवाय शुभम् शुभम् करु
transliteration: Om Namah Shivaya Shubham Shubham Kuru
english_translation: Om, I bow to Shiva — bring auspiciousness, bring auspiciousness.
phonetic_guide: OHM nah-MAH shih-VAH-yah SHOO-bahm SHOO-bahm KOO-roo
context_note: An expanded form of the Panchakshara invoking blessings and positive energy into daily life.
go_deeper_teaser: Morning mantras that shift your entire day →

english_translation: I am Shiva. I am Shiva.
context_note: From Adi Shankaracharya's Nirvana Shatakam. A direct affirmation of identity with pure consciousness.
go_deeper_teaser: The Nirvana Shatakam: Shankara's greatest poem →

english_translation: Om — the seed of Shiva — the seed of life — I am that.
context_note: The Maha Mrityunjaya bija mantra. Haum is the seed syllable of Shiva; Jum is the seed of life force.
go_deeper_teaser: Bija mantras: seeds of cosmic power →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Shiva, I bow.
context_note: An extended form adding a second namah — deepening the gesture of surrender and reverence.
go_deeper_teaser: What surrender really means in spiritual practice →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Shiva who brings peace and auspiciousness.
context_note: Sham is the bija of peace and cooling. This mantra soothes the nervous system and calms mental agitation.
go_deeper_teaser: How mantra activates the parasympathetic nervous system →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the union of Shiva and Shakti — consciousness and energy.
context_note: Shiva is pure awareness; Shakti is the dynamic energy of creation. Their union is the source of all existence.
go_deeper_teaser: The sacred marriage: Shiva and Shakti explained →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the Great God — the supreme deity.
context_note: Mahadeva means "Great God" — one of Shiva's most exalted names, honoring his supremacy among the devas.
go_deeper_teaser: The 108 names of Shiva and their meanings →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blue-throated one who swallowed poison to save the world.
context_note: Nilakantha — "blue throat" — refers to Shiva holding the cosmic poison in his throat to protect all beings. A mantra of fearless compassion.
go_deeper_teaser: The churning of the ocean: Shiva's greatest sacrifice →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the Lord of the Cosmic Dance.
context_note: Nataraja — Shiva as the cosmic dancer — represents the continuous cycles of creation, preservation, and dissolution.
go_deeper_teaser: Nataraja: the physics of the cosmic dance →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the Lord of all creatures.
context_note: Pashupati — "Lord of animals" — honors Shiva as the protector of all sentient beings, from the smallest creature to the greatest.
go_deeper_teaser: Shiva as the original animal protector →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the source of all existence.
context_note: Bhava means "being" or "existence." This name honors Shiva as the ground of all being — the very fact that anything exists at all.
go_deeper_teaser: The miracle of existence: why being here matters →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Rudra — the fierce one who removes suffering.
context_note: Rudra is Shiva's Vedic form — the howling storm god who destroys what no longer serves. Chanting this releases stuck energy.
go_deeper_teaser: Rudra: the sacred power of divine anger →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the one who brings blessings and auspiciousness.
context_note: Shankara — "conferring happiness" — is one of Shiva's most beloved names. This mantra opens the heart to receive grace.
go_deeper_teaser: Opening to grace: the practice of receptivity →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the Lord of the mountains.
context_note: Girisha — "mountain lord" — honors Shiva's home on Mount Kailash. Mountains represent the immovable foundation of consciousness.
go_deeper_teaser: Mount Kailash: the axis of the world →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the innocent lord — the simple-hearted one.
context_note: Bholenath — "innocent lord" — is a beloved folk name for Shiva. He is easily pleased by sincere devotion, however simple.
go_deeper_teaser: Why Shiva is the easiest god to please →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the Lord of the universe.
context_note: Vishwanath — "Lord of all worlds" — is the presiding deity of Varanasi, the oldest living city on earth.
go_deeper_teaser: Varanasi: the city where Shiva never sleeps →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the one who is quickly satisfied.
context_note: Ashutosh — "quickly pleased" — teaches that Shiva asks for nothing more than sincere love. Contentment is the highest offering.
go_deeper_teaser: The art of contentment: Santosha in daily life →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the three-eyed one who sees past, present, and future.
context_note: Shiva's third eye represents the eye of wisdom — the ability to see beyond duality into the nature of reality.
go_deeper_teaser: Activating your third eye through mantra →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the eternally auspicious one.
context_note: Sadashiva — "always auspicious" — represents the eternal, unchanging aspect of Shiva beyond time and space.
go_deeper_teaser: What it means to touch the eternal →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the consort of Uma — the divine feminine.
context_note: Uma (Parvati) is Shiva's beloved. This mantra honors the sacred partnership of masculine and feminine principles.
go_deeper_teaser: Shiva and Parvati: the original love story →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the matted-haired one who holds the Ganges.
context_note: Shiva's matted hair (jata) holds the sacred river Ganga, purifying all who touch her waters.
go_deeper_teaser: The Ganga in Shiva's hair: a story of grace →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the one adorned with sacred ash.
context_note: Shiva smears himself with ash from cremation grounds — a reminder that all bodies return to ash. This mantra cultivates non-attachment.
go_deeper_teaser: Sacred ash and the wisdom of impermanence →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the one who holds the sacred Ganga in his locks.
context_note: When the heavenly Ganga descended to earth, Shiva caught her in his hair to prevent the earth from being destroyed by her force.
go_deeper_teaser: How Shiva saved the earth with his hair →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the one with matted locks of an ascetic.
context_note: The matted hair (jata) represents the renunciation of worldly vanity and the accumulation of tapas — spiritual heat.
go_deeper_teaser: Tapas: the sacred fire of spiritual discipline →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the Lord of time who transcends all time.
context_note: Kaleshwara — "Lord of time" — reminds us that Shiva is both the master of time and beyond it. This mantra anchors us in the eternal now.
go_deeper_teaser: Living in the eternal now: Shiva's greatest teaching →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the ruler of the northeast — the direction of grace.
context_note: Ishana is Shiva's form ruling the northeast direction — associated with wisdom, grace, and the transmission of spiritual knowledge.
go_deeper_teaser: The five faces of Shiva: Panchamukha explained →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the supreme being — the highest self.
context_note: Tatpurusha — "that supreme person" — is the east-facing form of Shiva, representing the transcendent self beyond ego.
go_deeper_teaser: Tat tvam asi: you are that →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the non-terrible one — the gentle face of Shiva.
context_note: Aghora — "not terrible" — is the south-facing form of Shiva, representing the dissolution of ego and acceptance of all aspects of existence.
go_deeper_teaser: The Aghori path: embracing what we fear →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the beautiful god — the graceful one.
context_note: Vamadeva — "beautiful god" — is the north-facing form of Shiva, representing the creative, nurturing, and beautiful aspects of consciousness.
go_deeper_teaser: Beauty as a spiritual path →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the instantly born — the creative impulse of consciousness.
context_note: Sadyojata — "instantly born" — is the west-facing form of Shiva, representing the spontaneous creative power of consciousness.
go_deeper_teaser: Spontaneous creation: how inspiration really works →

english_translation: Om, remove, remove — O Great God, take away all that binds me.
context_note: Hara means "one who removes" — Shiva removes sin, suffering, and the illusion of separation. A powerful mantra of liberation.
go_deeper_teaser: What Shiva removes and why you should let him →

english_translation: Om, Shiva — the self-luminous, the source of joy.
context_note: Shambhu — "self-luminous" or "source of happiness" — is one of Shiva's most joyful names. This mantra invokes pure bliss.
go_deeper_teaser: Ananda: the bliss that is your true nature →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Shiva who is the teacher within.
context_note: The guru mantra of the Shaiva Siddhanta tradition. Shiva is the first guru — the inner teacher who guides all seekers.
go_deeper_teaser: The inner guru: how Shiva teaches from within →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Shiva the teacher.
context_note: The opening mantra of the Shiva Sutras tradition. Humility before the teacher opens the door to genuine wisdom.
go_deeper_teaser: The Shiva Sutras: Kashmir Shaivism's greatest text →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Shiva — peace, peace, peace.
context_note: The triple shanti at the end of Vedic recitations removes the three types of suffering: physical, mental, and cosmic.
go_deeper_teaser: The three types of suffering and how to end them →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Shiva — I offer this into the sacred fire.
context_note: Svaha is the offering call of fire rituals. Adding it transforms any mantra into a complete act of surrender and offering.
go_deeper_teaser: Homa: the ancient practice of fire offering →

original_script: ॐ शिव ॐ शिव ॐ शिव
transliteration: Om Shiva Om Shiva Om Shiva
english_translation: Om Shiva, Om Shiva, Om Shiva — pure consciousness, again and again.
phonetic_guide: OHM SHIH-vah OHM SHIH-vah OHM SHIH-vah
context_note: Repetition of the divine name is itself the practice. Three repetitions honor the trimurti and the three states of consciousness.
go_deeper_teaser: The power of divine name repetition →

original_script: ॐ नमः शिवाय शिवाय नमः ॐ
transliteration: Om Namah Shivaya Shivaya Namah Om
english_translation: Om, I bow to Shiva, to Shiva I bow, Om.
phonetic_guide: OHM nah-MAH shih-VAH-yah shih-VAH-yah nah-MAH OHM
context_note: A palindromic mantra — it reads the same forward and backward, representing the non-dual nature of Shiva consciousness.
go_deeper_teaser: Non-duality: the teaching that changes everything →

english_translation: Om, may we know Shiva.
context_note: The first line of the Shiva Gayatri — a request for direct knowledge of the divine, not merely belief or concept.
go_deeper_teaser: Direct knowing vs. belief: the Vedantic distinction →

english_translation: Om, may we know Shiva, may we meditate on the Great God.
context_note: The Shiva Gayatri — a request for illumination through meditation on Shiva's supreme nature.
go_deeper_teaser: The Gayatri structure: the most powerful mantra form →

english_translation: May Rudra illuminate and inspire us.
context_note: The concluding line of the Rudra Gayatri. Prachodayat — "may he inspire" — is the same word used in the Gayatri Mantra.
go_deeper_teaser: Prachodayat: the prayer for divine inspiration →

english_translation: Om, I am the principle of Shiva — pure consciousness.
context_note: A mahavakya-style affirmation from Kashmir Shaivism. Tattva means "that-ness" or essential principle. You are not separate from Shiva.
go_deeper_teaser: The mahavakyas: four great sayings of the Upanishads →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Shiva eternally — always and forever.
context_note: Nityam means "eternally" or "always." Adding this word transforms the mantra into a vow of perpetual devotion.
go_deeper_teaser: Making a spiritual vow: the power of commitment →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Narayana — the one who dwells in all beings.
context_note: The Ashtakshara — eight-syllable mantra of Vishnu. Narayana means "the one who is the abode of all beings."
go_deeper_teaser: Narayana: the god who lives inside you →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the Lord Vasudeva — the one who pervades all.
context_note: The Dvadasakshara — twelve-syllable mantra of Vishnu. Vasudeva means "the one who dwells everywhere." From the Bhagavata Purana.
go_deeper_teaser: The Bhagavata Purana: Vishnu's greatest scripture →

english_translation: O Hari, O Krishna — O divine energy, O all-attractive one.
context_note: The first half of the Maha Mantra. Hare addresses the divine energy (Radha); Krishna means "all-attractive." From the Kali Santarana Upanishad.
go_deeper_teaser: The Maha Mantra: why it works for everyone →

english_translation: O Hari, O Rama — O divine energy, O the one who gives joy.
context_note: The second half of the Maha Mantra. Rama means "the one who gives joy" or "the one in whom the yogis delight."
go_deeper_teaser: Rama and Krishna: two faces of the same light →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the radiant Rama — the ideal of righteousness.
context_note: Rama is the seventh avatar of Vishnu and the embodiment of dharma — right action, integrity, and noble character.
go_deeper_teaser: Dharma: the path of right action →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the radiant Krishna — the all-attractive divine.
context_note: Krishna is the eighth avatar of Vishnu and the teacher of the Bhagavad Gita. His name means "all-attractive" or "dark one."
go_deeper_teaser: The Bhagavad Gita in one sentence →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Vishnu — the all-pervading one.
context_note: Vishnu means "the all-pervading one" — the divine presence that permeates every atom of creation.
go_deeper_teaser: How to feel the divine presence everywhere →

english_translation: Om, may we know Narayana.
context_note: The opening of the Vishnu Gayatri — a prayer for direct knowledge of the all-pervading divine presence.
go_deeper_teaser: The Vishnu Gayatri: Vaishnava's most sacred prayer →

english_translation: Om, may we meditate on Vasudeva.
context_note: The second line of the Vishnu Gayatri. Dhimahi — "may we meditate" — invites sustained contemplation of the divine.
go_deeper_teaser: How to meditate on a divine form →

english_translation: May Vishnu illuminate and inspire us.
context_note: The concluding line of the Vishnu Gayatri. A prayer for divine illumination in all endeavors.
go_deeper_teaser: Vishnu's grace: how the preserver works in your life →

english_translation: Om, victory to the Lord of the universe who removes all suffering.
context_note: From the beloved Aarti prayer sung at Vishnu temples across India. Jagadish means "Lord of the world."
go_deeper_teaser: Aarti: the ritual of light and devotion →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the radiant, all-pervading Vishnu.
context_note: Shri (Lakshmi) is Vishnu's consort. Invoking both together brings the fullness of divine grace — both consciousness and abundance.
go_deeper_teaser: Lakshmi and Vishnu: the divine couple of abundance →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Hari — the one who removes all suffering.
context_note: Hari means "the remover" — Vishnu removes the three types of suffering: physical, mental, and karmic.
go_deeper_teaser: How Vishnu removes suffering: the path of grace →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Keshava — the one with beautiful hair.
context_note: Keshava — "beautiful-haired" or "slayer of Keshi" — is one of Krishna's most beloved names. Beauty is a form of the divine.
go_deeper_teaser: The 1000 names of Vishnu: the Vishnu Sahasranama →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Madhava — the lord of spring, the sweet one.
context_note: Madhava means "spring" or "sweet" — Krishna as the embodiment of sweetness, beauty, and the joy of creation.
go_deeper_teaser: Madhurya bhava: the sweetness of divine love →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Govinda — the protector of cows and the earth.
context_note: Govinda — "protector of cows" — is one of Krishna's most beloved names, honoring his childhood as a cowherd in Vrindavan.
go_deeper_teaser: Vrindavan: the sacred forest of Krishna's childhood →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Vishnu who took three steps to encompass the universe.
context_note: Trivikrama — "three steps" — refers to Vamana avatar who measured the entire universe in three strides. Consciousness is boundless.
go_deeper_teaser: Vamana: the dwarf who became the universe →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the infallible one who never falls.
context_note: Achyuta — "infallible" or "imperishable" — is one of Krishna's names in the Bhagavad Gita. The divine never wavers.
go_deeper_teaser: Steadiness in the storm: Arjuna's lesson →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the infinite, endless one.
context_note: Ananta — "infinite" — is both a name of Vishnu and the cosmic serpent on which he rests. Infinity is the ground of all existence.
go_deeper_teaser: Ananta Shesha: the serpent of eternity →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the lotus-eyed one.
context_note: Pundarikaksha — "lotus-eyed" — describes Vishnu's eyes as pure as lotus flowers. The lotus grows in mud but remains unstained.
go_deeper_teaser: The lotus: growing pure in an impure world →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the one who holds Shri — the divine abundance.
context_note: Sridhara — "holder of Shri" — honors Vishnu as the one who sustains Lakshmi, the goddess of abundance, on his chest.
go_deeper_teaser: Lakshmi on Vishnu's chest: the secret of abundance →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the lord of the senses.
context_note: Hrishikesha — "lord of the senses" — is the name Arjuna uses when asking Krishna to be his charioteer in the Bhagavad Gita.
go_deeper_teaser: Mastery of the senses: the Bhagavad Gita's teaching →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the one with a lotus in his navel — the source of creation.
context_note: From Vishnu's navel grows a lotus, and from that lotus emerges Brahma, the creator. The navel is the center of creative power.
go_deeper_teaser: The cosmic navel: creation from the center →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Damodara — the one bound by love.
context_note: Damodara — "rope around the belly" — refers to Krishna being tied by his mother Yashoda. Love is the only force that can bind the infinite.
go_deeper_teaser: How love binds even God: the Damodara story →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Sankarshana — the one who draws all toward the divine.
context_note: Sankarshana is one of the four vyuha (emanation) forms of Vishnu, representing the attractive power of divine love.
go_deeper_teaser: The four vyuhas: Vishnu's cosmic emanations →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Pradyumna — the supremely luminous one.
context_note: Pradyumna — "supremely luminous" — is the son of Krishna and Rukmini, representing the mind aspect of the divine.
go_deeper_teaser: The luminous mind: how consciousness illuminates →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Aniruddha — the unstoppable one.
context_note: Aniruddha — "unstoppable" — represents the ego aspect of the divine that cannot be obstructed. Your true nature cannot be stopped.
go_deeper_teaser: Your unstoppable nature: removing inner obstacles →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the supreme person — the highest self.
context_note: Purushottama — "supreme person" — is described in the 15th chapter of the Bhagavad Gita as the highest reality beyond both the perishable and imperishable.
go_deeper_teaser: The three purushhas: Gita chapter 15 explained →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the one who sustains the entire universe.
context_note: Vishwambhara — "sustainer of the universe" — reminds us that the same intelligence sustaining the cosmos is sustaining you.
go_deeper_teaser: Trusting the intelligence that runs the universe →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the one who gives joy to all people.
context_note: Janardana — "one who gives joy to people" — is the name used when Krishna acts as the divine servant of humanity.
go_deeper_teaser: Seva: the yoga of selfless service →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Upendra — the younger brother of Indra.
context_note: Upendra — "junior Indra" — refers to Vamana avatar. The infinite chose to appear as the smallest, most humble form to teach the greatest lesson.
go_deeper_teaser: The power of appearing small: Vamana's teaching →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Vamana — the dwarf avatar who measured the cosmos.
context_note: Vamana took three steps to measure earth, sky, and heaven — teaching that the infinite can appear in the smallest form.
go_deeper_teaser: Small appearances, infinite reality →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Shrinivasa — the abode of Shri (Lakshmi).
context_note: Shrinivasa — "dwelling place of Shri" — is the name of Vishnu at Tirupati, the most visited temple in the world.
go_deeper_teaser: Tirupati: why 100,000 people visit every day →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Ranganatha — the lord of the stage of creation.
context_note: Ranganatha — "lord of the stage" — is Vishnu reclining on Ananta at Srirangam. The universe is his stage; we are his players.
go_deeper_teaser: Srirangam: the largest functioning temple in the world →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Venkatesa — the lord of Venkata hill.
context_note: Venkatesa is Vishnu at Tirumala-Tirupati. Venkata means "destroyer of sins." This mantra is believed to grant all desires.
go_deeper_teaser: The seven hills of Tirupati and their meaning →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Gopala — the protector of cows and souls.
context_note: Gopala — "protector of cows" — is Krishna as the divine cowherd who tenderly cares for all beings under his protection.
go_deeper_teaser: The cow in Vedic tradition: why she is sacred →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Mukunda — the giver of liberation.
context_note: Mukunda — "giver of mukti (liberation)" — is one of Krishna's most powerful names. Chanting it is said to dissolve karmic bondage.
go_deeper_teaser: Mukti: the four types of liberation in Vedanta →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Madhusudhana — the slayer of the demon Madhu.
context_note: Madhusudhana — "slayer of Madhu" — refers to Vishnu defeating the demon of delusion. This mantra destroys inner demons of doubt and fear.
go_deeper_teaser: The inner demons: what Vishnu really slays →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Trivikrama — the one who strode across the three worlds.
context_note: Trivikrama's three steps represent past, present, and future — or earth, atmosphere, and heaven. Consciousness encompasses all.
go_deeper_teaser: The three worlds and how to inhabit them all →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Varaha — the boar avatar who rescued the earth.
context_note: Varaha lifted the earth from the cosmic ocean on his tusks. This mantra invokes the power to rescue what is precious from darkness.
go_deeper_teaser: The Varaha avatar: diving into darkness to save the earth →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Narasimha — the man-lion who protects devotees.
context_note: Narasimha burst from a pillar to protect Prahlada — teaching that the divine is present everywhere, in every atom, at every moment.
go_deeper_teaser: Prahlada's faith: the devotee who couldn't be killed →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Parashurama — the warrior sage with the axe.
context_note: Parashurama — "Rama with the axe" — is the sixth avatar who restored cosmic justice. Sometimes fierce action is the most compassionate response.
go_deeper_teaser: When compassion requires fierceness →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Balarama — the strong one, Krishna's elder brother.
context_note: Balarama — "strong Rama" — is the eighth avatar, representing the strength that supports and protects the divine play of consciousness.
go_deeper_teaser: Balarama: the strength behind Krishna's play →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Kalki — the future avatar who restores righteousness.
context_note: Kalki is the tenth and final avatar, yet to come — riding a white horse to restore dharma at the end of the Kali Yuga.
go_deeper_teaser: The Kali Yuga: are we living in the dark age? →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the ten avatars — the divine in all its forms.
context_note: The ten avatars of Vishnu trace the evolution of consciousness from fish to human — a map of spiritual evolution across cosmic time.
go_deeper_teaser: Darwin and the Dashavatara: evolution in the Vedas →

english_translation: Om — the seed of Saraswati, Lakshmi, and Kali — wisdom, abundance, and transformation.
context_note: Three bija mantras of the divine feminine: Aim (Saraswati/wisdom), Hrim (Lakshmi/heart), Klim (Kali/transformation). Together they invoke the full power of Shakti.
go_deeper_teaser: The three great goddesses and their bija mantras →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Durga — the invincible one who protects.
context_note: Durga — "the invincible" or "the one who is difficult to approach" — is the warrior goddess who defeats all forms of evil and ignorance.
go_deeper_teaser: Durga's nine forms: the Navadurga →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Kali — the dark mother who liberates through transformation.
context_note: Kali — "the dark one" or "time" — is the most powerful form of Shakti. She destroys ego and illusion to reveal the eternal self beneath.
go_deeper_teaser: Kali: the goddess who loves you enough to destroy your ego →

english_translation: Om — Lakshmi's seed — the heart — Lakshmi's seed again.
context_note: Shrim is Lakshmi's bija — the seed of abundance, beauty, and grace. Hrim is the heart bija. Together they open the heart to receive.
go_deeper_teaser: Lakshmi's eight forms: the Ashtalakshmi →

english_translation: Om, with the seed of wisdom, I bow to Saraswati.
context_note: Saraswati — "the flowing one" — is the goddess of wisdom, learning, music, and the arts. Aim is her bija, activating the intellect.
go_deeper_teaser: Saraswati's four arms: the tools of wisdom →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the great Lakshmi — the goddess of all abundance.
context_note: Mahalakshmi — "great Lakshmi" — is the supreme form of the goddess of abundance, encompassing all eight forms of wealth.
go_deeper_teaser: The eight forms of Lakshmi and what they offer →

english_translation: Om, with the seed of transformation, I bow to Kali.
context_note: Klim is the bija of attraction and transformation. Combined with Kali's name, it invokes the power to release what no longer serves.
go_deeper_teaser: What Kali wants you to release →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Chandika — the fierce, passionate one.
context_note: Chandika is the fierce form of Durga from the Devi Mahatmya. She embodies the passionate, unstoppable force of the divine feminine.
go_deeper_teaser: The Devi Mahatmya: the goddess's greatest battle →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Tripurasundari — the beauty of the three worlds.
context_note: Tripurasundari — "beautiful in the three worlds" — is the supreme goddess of the Sri Vidya tradition, presiding over the Sri Yantra.
go_deeper_teaser: Sri Vidya: the highest Shakta tradition →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Bhuvaneshwari — the queen of the universe.
context_note: Bhuvaneshwari — "queen of the universe" — is the fourth of the ten Mahavidyas, representing the vast space of consciousness.
go_deeper_teaser: The ten Mahavidyas: the wisdom goddesses →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Bhairavi — the fierce, terrifying goddess of liberation.
context_note: Bhairavi — "the terrifying one" — is the fifth Mahavidya. Her terror is only for the ego; to the true self, she is pure love.
go_deeper_teaser: What we fear and why the goddess is not it →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Chhinnamasta — the self-decapitated goddess of sacrifice.
context_note: Chhinnamasta holds her own severed head, feeding her devotees with her blood. She teaches that the highest love is total self-giving.
go_deeper_teaser: The mystery of Chhinnamasta: love beyond the ego →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Dhumavati — the smoky widow goddess of dissolution.
context_note: Dhumavati — "the smoky one" — represents the void after dissolution. She teaches acceptance of loss, old age, and the wisdom of emptiness.
go_deeper_teaser: The wisdom of emptiness: Dhumavati's gift →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Bagalamukhi — the crane-faced goddess who paralyzes enemies.
context_note: Bagalamukhi — "crane-faced" — is the eighth Mahavidya who paralyzes negative forces. She stills the tongue of slander and the mind of confusion.
go_deeper_teaser: Bagalamukhi: the goddess who silences enemies →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Matangi — the outcast goddess of inner wisdom.
context_note: Matangi — the "outcast" or "elephant" goddess — is the ninth Mahavidya, representing wisdom that comes from outside conventional boundaries.
go_deeper_teaser: Matangi: the wisdom of the outcast →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Kamala — the lotus goddess, the tantric Lakshmi.
context_note: Kamala — "lotus" — is the tenth Mahavidya, the tantric form of Lakshmi. She represents the fullness of grace and the beauty of pure consciousness.
go_deeper_teaser: The ten Mahavidyas: a complete map of divine feminine power →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Tara — the star goddess who guides across the ocean of existence.
context_note: Tara — "star" or "one who saves" — is the second Mahavidya and also a major Buddhist deity. She guides souls across the ocean of samsara.
go_deeper_teaser: Tara: the goddess shared by Hinduism and Buddhism →

original_script: ॐ ऐं ह्रीं श्रीं त्रिपुर सुन्दरी
transliteration: Om Aim Hrim Shrim Tripura Sundari
english_translation: Om — wisdom, heart, abundance — beauty of the three worlds.
phonetic_guide: OHM AIM hreem shreem tree-POO-rah soon-DAH-ree
context_note: Day 108 — the sacred number of the mala. This mantra combines the three great bija mantras with the supreme goddess of the Sri Yantra.
go_deeper_teaser: Why 108 is the most sacred number →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the goddess, the great goddess.
context_note: The opening invocation of the Devi Mahatmya — the most sacred text of the Shakta tradition, celebrating the goddess's cosmic victory.
go_deeper_teaser: The Devi Mahatmya: 700 verses of goddess power →

english_translation: The goddess who dwells in all beings...
context_note: From the Devi Mahatmya. The full verse continues: "...in the form of consciousness, power, peace, faith, beauty, activity, memory, compassion, and error — I bow to her."
go_deeper_teaser: The goddess in all forms: the Devi Mahatmya's teaching →

english_translation: I bow to her, I bow to her, I bow to her, I bow.
context_note: The refrain of the Devi Mahatmya. Triple repetition of surrender to the goddess — a complete act of devotion.
go_deeper_teaser: The power of triple repetition in mantra →

english_translation: Om, power, power, power — the divine feminine energy.
context_note: Shakti is the primordial energy of the universe — the force that animates all of creation. This mantra awakens your own inner Shakti.
go_deeper_teaser: Kundalini Shakti: the sleeping serpent power →

english_translation: Om, victory to the Divine Mother.
context_note: The simplest and most heartfelt invocation of the Divine Mother. Maa — "mother" — is the most intimate name for the goddess.
go_deeper_teaser: The Divine Mother: the most intimate face of God →

english_translation: Om, with the seed of Ganesha, I bow to the lord of all beginnings.
context_note: Gam is Ganesha's bija mantra. Ganapati — "lord of the ganas" — is invoked at the beginning of every undertaking to remove obstacles.
go_deeper_teaser: Why Ganesha is invoked first in every ritual →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Ganesha — the lord of hosts.
context_note: Ganesha — "lord of the ganas" — is the remover of obstacles and the bestower of wisdom. He is invoked before all sacred activities.
go_deeper_teaser: Ganesha's elephant head: the wisdom of the big picture →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the king of obstacles — who removes all impediments.
context_note: Vighnaraja — "king of obstacles" — is Ganesha's paradoxical title. He both creates and removes obstacles, teaching that challenges are divine gifts.
go_deeper_teaser: Why Ganesha creates obstacles before removing them →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the single-tusked one — the one who focuses on the essential.
context_note: Ekadanta — "single tusk" — refers to Ganesha breaking off one tusk to write the Mahabharata. He teaches us to sacrifice the non-essential for the essential.
go_deeper_teaser: Why Ganesha broke his tusk to write the Mahabharata →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the large-bellied one who contains all universes.
context_note: Lambodara — "large belly" — represents Ganesha's capacity to contain all of existence. His belly holds the entire cosmos.
go_deeper_teaser: Ganesha's belly: the universe within →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Vinayaka — the supreme leader who guides without being led.
context_note: Vinayaka — "supreme leader" or "one who has no master" — is Ganesha's most common name in South India.
go_deeper_teaser: True leadership: the Vinayaka principle →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the elephant-faced one.
context_note: Gajanana — "elephant-faced" — honors Ganesha's elephant head, which represents wisdom, memory, and the ability to remove obstacles with gentle power.
go_deeper_teaser: The elephant's wisdom: what Ganesha's head teaches →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the lord of all groups and communities.
context_note: Ganadhipa — "lord of groups" — honors Ganesha as the presiding deity of all communities, councils, and collective endeavors.
go_deeper_teaser: Sangha: the power of spiritual community →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Siddhivinayaka — the bestower of all accomplishments.
context_note: Siddhivinayaka — "bestower of siddhi" — is the most popular form of Ganesha in Mumbai. Siddhi means accomplishment, perfection, and supernatural power.
go_deeper_teaser: The eight siddhis: spiritual powers and how to use them →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the one who loves intelligence.
context_note: Buddhipriya — "lover of intelligence" — honors Ganesha as the patron of learning, scholarship, and all forms of intellectual endeavor.
go_deeper_teaser: Ganesha as the patron of learning: the student's mantra →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the lord of all knowledge.
context_note: Vidyadhipa — "lord of knowledge" — honors Ganesha as the supreme patron of all forms of learning, from the sacred to the mundane.
go_deeper_teaser: Para vidya and apara vidya: two kinds of knowledge →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the one whose vehicle is the mouse.
context_note: Ganesha rides a mouse — the smallest of vehicles for the largest of gods. The mouse represents the ego: small, quick, and capable of gnawing through obstacles.
go_deeper_teaser: Why Ganesha rides a mouse: the paradox of power →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the one who loves modaka — the sweet of bliss.
context_note: Modaka — a sweet rice dumpling — is Ganesha's favorite offering. It represents the sweetness of spiritual realization.
go_deeper_teaser: Offering to Ganesha: the art of sacred giving →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Heramba — the protector of the weak.
context_note: Heramba — "protector of the weak" — is a five-headed form of Ganesha who specifically protects those who are vulnerable or in difficulty.
go_deeper_teaser: Heramba: Ganesha's fierce protective form →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the great lord of all hosts.
context_note: Mahaganapataye — "great Ganapati" — is the supreme form of Ganesha, combining all his powers and aspects into one.
go_deeper_teaser: The 32 forms of Ganesha: a complete guide →

english_translation: Om, may we know Ganesha.
context_note: The opening of the Ganesha Gayatri — a prayer for direct knowledge of the remover of obstacles.
go_deeper_teaser: The Ganesha Gayatri: the most powerful Ganesha mantra →

english_translation: Om, may we meditate on the curved-trunk one.
context_note: Vakratunda — "curved trunk" — honors Ganesha's trunk, which curves to pick up the sweetness of life. The second line of the Ganesha Gayatri.
go_deeper_teaser: Ganesha's trunk: the power of discernment →

english_translation: May the tusked one inspire and illuminate us.
context_note: The concluding line of the Ganesha Gayatri. Danti — "tusked one" — refers to Ganesha's single tusk, the pen that wrote the Mahabharata.
go_deeper_teaser: The Mahabharata: the world's longest poem →

english_translation: O curved-trunk one, O great-bodied one...
context_note: The opening of the most popular Ganesha shloka. The full verse: "O curved-trunk, great-bodied, brilliant as a million suns — remove all obstacles from my work, always."
go_deeper_teaser: The Vakratunda shloka: Ganesha's most popular prayer →

english_translation: Brilliant as ten million suns.
context_note: The second line of the Vakratunda shloka. Ganesha's radiance is compared to ten million suns — the light of consciousness itself.
go_deeper_teaser: The light of consciousness: what Ganesha's radiance means →

english_translation: O God, make my work free from obstacles.
context_note: The third line of the Vakratunda shloka — a direct prayer for obstacle-free action. Nirvighna means "without obstacles."
go_deeper_teaser: Removing obstacles: the Ganesha principle in action →

english_translation: In all endeavors, always.
context_note: The final line of the Vakratunda shloka. Sarvada — "always" — transforms the prayer into a constant, living invocation.
go_deeper_teaser: Living in constant prayer: the practice of remembrance →

original_script: ॐ गं ॐ गं ॐ गं
transliteration: Om Gam Om Gam Om Gam
english_translation: Om, Ganesha's seed — three times — opening all doors.
phonetic_guide: OHM gahm OHM gahm OHM gahm
context_note: The simplest Ganesha practice: just his bija, three times. Gam activates the root chakra and opens the path ahead.
go_deeper_teaser: The root chakra and Ganesha: the foundation of practice →

english_translation: I recognize and embody the eternal light that lives within me.
context_note: A powerful affirmation rooted in Vedantic truth — you are not the body, not the mind, but the luminous awareness that witnesses all.
go_deeper_teaser: Why recognizing your inner light changes everything →

english_translation: That divine solar radiance which is most adorable.
context_note: The second line of the Gayatri Mantra. Savitri is the solar deity — the divine light that illuminates both the outer sun and the inner light of consciousness.
go_deeper_teaser: Savitri: the solar deity behind the Gayatri →

english_translation: May we meditate on the divine radiance.
context_note: The third line of the Gayatri. Bhargo means "radiance" or "splendor" — the light that burns away ignorance.
go_deeper_teaser: Bhargo: the light that destroys darkness →

english_translation: May that divine light inspire and illuminate our intellect.
context_note: The final line of the Gayatri Mantra. Dhiyo — "intellect" — is what we offer to the divine light for purification and illumination.
go_deeper_teaser: The Gayatri Mantra: the most powerful mantra in the Vedas →

english_translation: I am inherently worthy of giving and receiving boundless love.
context_note: Love is not something you earn — it is your birthright. This affirmation dissolves the false belief that you must perform to be loved.
go_deeper_teaser: The spiritual truth about self-worth →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Surya — the solar deity, the source of all light.
context_note: Surya is the solar deity — the visible form of the divine. The sun is the source of all life on earth and a symbol of the inner light of consciousness.
go_deeper_teaser: Surya Namaskar: the sun salutation as prayer →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Aditya — the son of Aditi, the infinite.
context_note: Aditya — "son of Aditi" — is the sun as the child of the infinite. Aditi means "the boundless" — the cosmic mother of all the gods.
go_deeper_teaser: Aditi: the infinite mother of the gods →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Mitra — the sun as the universal friend.
context_note: Mitra — "friend" — is the sun as the universal benefactor. The sun shines equally on all without discrimination.
go_deeper_teaser: Mitra: the sun as the friend of all beings →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Ravi — the radiant one.
context_note: Ravi — "the radiant" — is one of the twelve names of the sun recited during Surya Namaskar. Radiance is the sun's essential nature.
go_deeper_teaser: The 12 names of the sun: the Surya Namaskar mantras →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Surya, I bow to Bhaskara — the one who illuminates.
context_note: Bhaskara — "the illuminator" — is the sun as the force that makes all things visible and knowable.
go_deeper_teaser: Light as a metaphor for consciousness →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Khaga — the one who moves through the sky.
context_note: Khaga — "sky-mover" — honors the sun's daily journey across the heavens. The sun's freedom of movement is a symbol of liberated consciousness.
go_deeper_teaser: The sun's daily journey: a meditation on freedom →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Pushan — the nourisher of all.
context_note: Pushan — "the nourisher" — is the sun as the source of all food and sustenance. The sun nourishes every living being without exception.
go_deeper_teaser: Pushan: the sun as the cosmic nourisher →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Hiranyagarbha — the golden womb of creation.
context_note: Hiranyagarbha — "golden womb" — is the cosmic egg from which the universe was born. The sun is its visible manifestation.
go_deeper_teaser: Hiranyagarbha: the golden egg of creation →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Marichi — the ray of light.
context_note: Marichi — "ray of light" — honors the individual rays of the sun. Each ray is both part of the sun and distinct from it — like each soul in relation to the divine.
go_deeper_teaser: The ray and the sun: the relationship between soul and God →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Aditya-Savitri — the solar deity who animates all life.
context_note: Savitri — "the animating one" — is the sun as the force that animates all living beings. Without the sun, there is no life.
go_deeper_teaser: Prana: the solar life force within you →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Divakara — the maker of the day.
context_note: Divakara — "day-maker" — honors the sun as the creator of each new day. Every sunrise is a new beginning, a fresh opportunity.
go_deeper_teaser: The gift of each new day: a morning practice →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Prabhakara — the maker of light.
context_note: Prabhakara — "maker of light" — honors the sun as the source of all illumination, both physical and spiritual.
go_deeper_teaser: Enlightenment: what it really means →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Vivasvat — the brilliant, shining one.
context_note: Vivasvat — "the brilliant" — is the Vedic name of the sun. In the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna says he first taught this yoga to Vivasvat.
go_deeper_teaser: The eternal yoga: Krishna's teaching to the sun →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Savitri — the solar deity of the Gayatri.
context_note: Savitri is the specific solar deity invoked in the Gayatri Mantra. The name means "one who inspires" or "one who animates."
go_deeper_teaser: Savitri: the goddess behind the Gayatri Mantra →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Arka — the sun as the worthy of praise.
context_note: Arka — "worthy of praise" — honors the sun as the most visible and constant blessing in our lives. Gratitude for the sun is gratitude for life itself.
go_deeper_teaser: Gratitude as a spiritual practice →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Bhanu — the luminous one.
context_note: Bhanu — "luminous" — is one of the twelve names of the sun. Luminosity is the sun's nature; it cannot help but shine.
go_deeper_teaser: Your natural luminosity: the practice of shining →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Indra — the king of the gods, lord of lightning.
context_note: Indra is the king of the Vedic gods, wielder of the thunderbolt (vajra). He represents the power of the awakened mind.
go_deeper_teaser: Indra and the thunderbolt: the power of awakening →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Varuna — the lord of cosmic order and the waters.
context_note: Varuna is the Vedic god of cosmic order (rita) and the waters. He sees all actions and maintains the moral order of the universe.
go_deeper_teaser: Rita: the cosmic order that underlies all existence →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Agni — the sacred fire, the divine messenger.
context_note: Agni is the first word of the Rigveda. The sacred fire is the divine messenger between humans and gods, and the purifier of all offerings.
go_deeper_teaser: Agni: the first word of the Vedas →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Vayu — the wind, the breath of life.
context_note: Vayu — "wind" or "breath" — is the Vedic god of air and prana. The breath is the most direct link between the physical and spiritual.
go_deeper_teaser: Pranayama: the yoga of the breath →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Prithivi — the earth, our mother.
context_note: Prithivi — "the broad one" — is the Vedic earth goddess. The earth supports all life without discrimination. Grounding is the foundation of all practice.
go_deeper_teaser: Prithivi: the earth as the divine mother →

english_translation: Om, may the waters bring peace.
context_note: From the Shanti Patha — the Vedic peace invocation. The waters (apah) represent the flow of life and the cleansing power of grace.
go_deeper_teaser: The Shanti Patha: the Vedic prayer for universal peace →

english_translation: Om, may the herbs and plants bring peace.
context_note: From the Shanti Patha. The healing plants of the earth are invoked for peace — honoring the medicinal wisdom of the natural world.
go_deeper_teaser: Ayurveda: the Vedic science of healing →

english_translation: O divine Savitri, remove all that is harmful from us.
context_note: From the Yajurveda. A prayer to the solar deity to remove all that is harmful and bring only what is beneficial.
go_deeper_teaser: The Yajurveda: the Veda of ritual action →

english_translation: Remove all our sins and shortcomings.
context_note: The second line of the Vishwani prayer. Duritani means "that which causes pain" — the prayer asks for removal of all inner obstacles.
go_deeper_teaser: Forgiveness as a spiritual practice →

english_translation: And bring to us all that is auspicious.
context_note: The final line of the Vishwani prayer. Bhadram — "auspicious" — is the Vedic word for all that is good, beautiful, and beneficial.
go_deeper_teaser: Bhadram: the Vedic vision of the good life →

english_translation: Om, salutation to the sun.
context_note: Surya Namaskar — "sun salutation" — is both a physical yoga practice and a devotional act. Each movement is accompanied by a mantra to the sun.
go_deeper_teaser: The complete Surya Namaskar practice →

original_script: ॐ ह्रां ह्रीं ह्रौं सः सूर्याय नमः
transliteration: Om Hram Hrim Hraum Sah Suryaya Namah
english_translation: Om — the three solar bija mantras — I am that — I bow to Surya.
phonetic_guide: OHM hrahm hreem hrowm sah SOOR-yah-yah nah-MAH
context_note: The complete solar bija mantra. Hram, Hrim, Hraum are the three seed syllables of the sun, representing its three aspects: rising, noon, and setting.
go_deeper_teaser: Solar bija mantras: seeds of solar power →

english_translation: Om, the one who resembles the hibiscus flower.
context_note: The opening of the Aditya Hridayam — the solar hymn from the Ramayana. The sun is compared to the red hibiscus flower at dawn.
go_deeper_teaser: The Aditya Hridayam: the heart of the sun →

english_translation: The son of Kashyapa, of great radiance.
context_note: The second line of the Aditya Hridayam. The sun is the son of the sage Kashyapa — reminding us that even the sun has a lineage and a teacher.
go_deeper_teaser: Kashyapa: the progenitor of all life →

english_translation: The enemy of darkness, the destroyer of all sins.
context_note: The third line of the Aditya Hridayam. The sun destroys both physical darkness and the inner darkness of ignorance and sin.
go_deeper_teaser: The Aditya Hridayam: how Rama defeated Ravana →

english_translation: I bow to the maker of the day.
context_note: The final line of the Aditya Hridayam opening verse. Pranata — "bowing" — is the posture of humility before the divine light.
go_deeper_teaser: Pranama: the practice of sacred bowing →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the peaceful sun.
context_note: The sun is peaceful — it shines without effort, without preference, without agitation. This mantra invokes solar equanimity.
go_deeper_teaser: Equanimity: the sun's greatest teaching →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the sun who gives everything.
context_note: The sun gives its light freely to all — the saint and the sinner, the rich and the poor. This mantra invokes divine generosity.
go_deeper_teaser: The sun's generosity: giving without expectation →

english_translation: Om, I bow to you, O solar deity.
context_note: A simple, direct salutation to the sun. Namostu — "may there be bowing" — expresses perpetual reverence.
go_deeper_teaser: Daily sun practice: the simplest solar sadhana →

english_translation: Om, be pleased, be pleased, O Lord.
context_note: Prasida — "be pleased" or "be gracious" — is a prayer for divine grace. The triple repetition intensifies the request.
go_deeper_teaser: Prasada: the grace that flows from the divine →

english_translation: Om, salutation to all the gods.
context_note: A universal salutation to all divine forms. The Vedas teach that all gods are aspects of the one divine reality.
go_deeper_teaser: Ekam sat vipra bahudha vadanti: one truth, many names →

english_translation: Reaches Keshava (the supreme).
context_note: The second line of the universal salutation verse: "Salutation to all gods reaches the supreme." All paths lead to the same destination.
go_deeper_teaser: All paths lead to the same peak →

english_translation: Om, you are radiance — place that radiance in me.
context_note: From the Yajurveda. A prayer to receive the sun's radiance — not just its physical light, but its inner quality of brilliance and clarity.
go_deeper_teaser: Tejas: the inner radiance of the awakened person →

english_translation: Om, you are vitality — place that vitality in me.
context_note: From the Yajurveda. Virya — "vitality" or "heroic energy" — is the solar quality of dynamic, purposeful action.
go_deeper_teaser: Virya: the heroic energy of the spiritual warrior →

english_translation: Om, you are strength — place that strength in me.
context_note: From the Yajurveda. Bala — "strength" — is the solar quality of physical and spiritual fortitude. The sun never wavers; neither should we.
go_deeper_teaser: Bala: the strength that comes from practice →

english_translation: Om — the jewel in the lotus — hum.
context_note: The mantra of Avalokiteshvara — the bodhisattva of compassion. The most widely chanted mantra in the world. The jewel is the awakened mind; the lotus is the world.
go_deeper_teaser: Om Mani Padme Hum: the six syllables of compassion →

english_translation: Om — Tara who liberates — Tara who liberates swiftly — Tara who protects — so be it.
context_note: The mantra of Green Tara — the swift-acting bodhisattva who saves from the eight fears. Tare means "she who liberates."
go_deeper_teaser: Green Tara: the swift liberator →

english_translation: Om — the heart of Amitabha — the buddha of infinite light.
context_note: Amitabha — "infinite light" — is the buddha of the western pure land. His light reaches all beings without exception.
go_deeper_teaser: Amitabha Buddha: the pure land of infinite light →

english_translation: I take refuge in Amitabha — the buddha of infinite light.
context_note: The nembutsu — the central practice of Pure Land Buddhism. Reciting Amitabha's name with sincere faith is said to ensure rebirth in the pure land.
go_deeper_teaser: The nembutsu: the simplest Buddhist practice →

original_script: ॐ अह हूँ वज्र गुरु पद्म सिद्धि हूँ
transliteration: Om Ah Hum Vajra Guru Padma Siddhi Hum
english_translation: Om Ah Hum — Vajra Guru — lotus-born — accomplishment — Hum.
phonetic_guide: OHM ah hoom VAHJ-rah GOO-roo PAHD-mah SID-dhee hoom
context_note: The mantra of Padmasambhava — Guru Rinpoche — who brought Buddhism to Tibet. The most important mantra in Vajrayana Buddhism.
go_deeper_teaser: Padmasambhava: the lotus-born master of Tibet →

english_translation: Om — Vajrasattva — the diamond being — Hum.
context_note: The short mantra of Vajrasattva — the buddha of purification. Vajra means "diamond" or "thunderbolt" — indestructible and pure.
go_deeper_teaser: Vajrasattva: the 100-syllable purification practice →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Manjushri — the bodhisattva of wisdom.
context_note: Manjushri holds a flaming sword to cut through ignorance and a book of wisdom. He is the embodiment of prajna — transcendent wisdom.
go_deeper_teaser: Manjushri's sword: cutting through ignorance →

original_script: ॐ अ रा पा चा ना धीः
transliteration: Om A Ra Pa Ca Na Dhih
english_translation: Om — the five wisdom syllables — the seed of wisdom.
phonetic_guide: OHM ah rah pah chah nah dhee
context_note: The mantra of Manjushri. Each syllable represents a wisdom teaching. Dhih is the seed syllable of wisdom — the most important syllable.
go_deeper_teaser: The five wisdom syllables of Manjushri →

original_script: ॐ गते गते पारगते पारसंगते बोधि स्वाहा
transliteration: Om Gate Gate Paragate Parasamgate Bodhi Svaha
english_translation: Gone, gone, gone beyond, gone completely beyond — awakening — so be it.
phonetic_guide: OHM GAH-tay GAH-tay pah-rah-GAH-tay pah-rah-sahm-GAH-tay BOH-dhee svah-HAH
context_note: The dharani from the Heart Sutra — the most concise expression of the Buddha's teaching on emptiness and transcendence.
go_deeper_teaser: The Heart Sutra: the Buddha's teaching on emptiness →

english_translation: I take refuge in the Buddha.
context_note: The first of the Three Jewels — the triple refuge that is the foundation of all Buddhist practice. The Buddha is the awakened teacher.
go_deeper_teaser: The Three Jewels: the foundation of Buddhist practice →

english_translation: I take refuge in the Dharma — the teaching.
context_note: The second of the Three Jewels. Dharma is the Buddha's teaching — the path that leads to liberation from suffering.
go_deeper_teaser: The Dharma: what the Buddha actually taught →

english_translation: I take refuge in the Sangha — the community.
context_note: The third of the Three Jewels. Sangha is the community of practitioners — those who walk the path together.
go_deeper_teaser: Sangha: why community is essential for awakening →

english_translation: May all beings be happy.
context_note: The metta (loving-kindness) prayer — the foundation of Buddhist compassion practice. It begins with all beings and works inward.
go_deeper_teaser: Metta meditation: the practice of loving-kindness →

english_translation: May all beings be free from suffering.
context_note: The second line of the metta prayer. Niramaya — "free from disease" — extends to all forms of suffering, physical and mental.
go_deeper_teaser: Karuna: the practice of compassion →

english_translation: May all beings be at ease.
context_note: The third line of the metta prayer. Sukhita — "at ease" — is a deeper wish than happiness: the ease of a mind free from struggle.
go_deeper_teaser: Upekkha: the practice of equanimity →

english_translation: Om, may all beings be well.
context_note: A universal Buddhist blessing. Svasti — "well-being" — is the root of the Sanskrit word swastika, an ancient symbol of auspiciousness.
go_deeper_teaser: Svasti: the original meaning of the swastika →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the Buddha — the awakened one.
context_note: A simple, direct invocation of the Buddha. Buddha means "the awakened one" — one who has seen through the illusion of the separate self.
go_deeper_teaser: What does it mean to be awakened? →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the Dharma — the truth.
context_note: Bowing to the Dharma is bowing to truth itself — the way things actually are, beyond our concepts and preferences.
go_deeper_teaser: The Dharma: the Buddha's four noble truths →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the Sangha — the community of practitioners.
context_note: The sangha is not just monks — it is all beings who walk the path of awakening. We are never alone on this journey.
go_deeper_teaser: The bodhisattva vow: awakening for all beings →

english_translation: Om — the infallible lasso of compassion — Hum.
context_note: Amoghapasha — "infallible lasso" — is a form of Avalokiteshvara who catches all beings in the net of compassion, leaving none behind.
go_deeper_teaser: Avalokiteshvara: the bodhisattva who hears all cries →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blessed Amitabha.
context_note: The full refuge mantra of Amitabha Buddha. Bhagavate — "blessed one" — is the honorific used for all buddhas.
go_deeper_teaser: The pure land: is it real or metaphorical? →

english_translation: Om — loving-kindness, loving-kindness, loving-kindness.
context_note: Metta (Pali) or Maitri (Sanskrit) — loving-kindness — is the first of the four brahmaviharas, the divine abodes. Triple repetition deepens the intention.
go_deeper_teaser: The four brahmaviharas: the divine abodes →

english_translation: Om — compassion, compassion, compassion.
context_note: Karuna — compassion — is the second brahmavihara. It is the wish for all beings to be free from suffering.
go_deeper_teaser: Karuna: the difference between compassion and pity →

english_translation: Om — sympathetic joy, sympathetic joy, sympathetic joy.
context_note: Mudita — "sympathetic joy" — is the third brahmavihara: the ability to rejoice in the happiness of others without envy.
go_deeper_teaser: Mudita: the antidote to envy →

english_translation: Om — equanimity, equanimity, equanimity.
context_note: Upekkha — equanimity — is the fourth brahmavihara: the ability to remain balanced and undisturbed in all circumstances.
go_deeper_teaser: Equanimity: the highest of the four divine abodes →

english_translation: Om — peace, peace, peace.
context_note: The universal peace invocation. Three repetitions address the three sources of disturbance: body, speech, and mind.
go_deeper_teaser: The three levels of peace: body, speech, and mind →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the three jewels.
context_note: Ratnatraya — "three jewels" — are the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha. Taking refuge in them is the foundation of all Buddhist practice.
go_deeper_teaser: Taking refuge: what it really means →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blessed Shakyamuni — the sage of the Shakya clan.
context_note: Shakyamuni — "sage of the Shakyas" — is the historical Buddha, Siddhartha Gautama. This mantra honors the human teacher who showed the way.
go_deeper_teaser: The historical Buddha: his life and awakening →

english_translation: Om — body, speech, mind — Hum.
context_note: The three seed syllables of Vajrayana Buddhism: Om purifies the body, Ah purifies speech, Hum purifies the mind.
go_deeper_teaser: Om Ah Hum: the three vajras of body, speech, and mind →

english_translation: Om — Vajrasattva — the sacred commitment.
context_note: The opening of the Vajrasattva 100-syllable mantra. Samaya — "sacred commitment" — is the vow between student and teacher, between practitioner and the divine.
go_deeper_teaser: Samaya: the sacred vow of Vajrayana →

english_translation: Please uphold me.
context_note: From the Vajrasattva mantra. A prayer for the divine to uphold and sustain the practitioner on the path.
go_deeper_teaser: The 100-syllable Vajrasattva mantra: complete guide →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Manjushri — the gentle-voiced one.
context_note: Manjushri — "gentle glory" — is the bodhisattva of wisdom. His name means "gentle-voiced" — wisdom is always gentle, never harsh.
go_deeper_teaser: Manjushri: the bodhisattva of wisdom →

english_translation: Om, I bow to Vajrapani — the thunderbolt-wielder.
context_note: Vajrapani — "thunderbolt in hand" — is the bodhisattva of power. He represents the power of the awakened mind to cut through all obstacles.
go_deeper_teaser: Vajrapani: the power of the awakened mind →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blessed Medicine Buddha.
context_note: Bhaishajyaguru — "Medicine Buddha" — is the buddha of healing. His body is lapis lazuli blue, and he holds a medicine bowl.
go_deeper_teaser: Medicine Buddha: the healing practice →

english_translation: To the king of lapis lazuli light.
context_note: Part of the Medicine Buddha mantra. Vaidurya — "lapis lazuli" — is the deep blue stone that represents the clarity of the awakened mind.
go_deeper_teaser: Lapis lazuli: the stone of the Medicine Buddha →

english_translation: To the thus-gone one — the one who has gone to suchness.
context_note: Tathagata — "thus-gone" or "thus-come" — is the Buddha's own name for himself. It points to the nature of reality: things are just as they are.
go_deeper_teaser: Tathata: the Buddhist concept of suchness →

english_translation: Om — eliminate, eliminate — the pain of illness.
context_note: The heart of the Medicine Buddha mantra. Bekandze means "eliminate pain" — the mantra is said to heal both physical illness and the illness of ignorance.
go_deeper_teaser: The Medicine Buddha mantra: healing body and mind →

english_translation: Great elimination — king — the supreme arising — so be it.
context_note: The conclusion of the Medicine Buddha mantra. Samudgate — "supreme arising" — points to the arising of the awakened mind that heals all.
go_deeper_teaser: The complete Medicine Buddha practice →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blessed one of infinite life.
context_note: Amitayus — "infinite life" — is the longevity aspect of Amitabha Buddha. This mantra is chanted for long life and the extension of the teaching.
go_deeper_teaser: Amitayus: the Buddha of infinite life →

english_translation: Om — immortal life — living — so be it.
context_note: The Amitayus mantra. Amarani — "immortal" — refers not to physical immortality but to the deathless nature of the awakened mind.
go_deeper_teaser: The deathless: what the Buddha meant by nirvana →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the one who purifies all lower realms.
context_note: The opening of the Stainless Beam mantra — one of the most powerful purification mantras in Tibetan Buddhism.
go_deeper_teaser: The Stainless Beam mantra: purifying all karma →

english_translation: Om, I generate the awakening mind.
context_note: Bodhicitta — "awakening mind" — is the aspiration to achieve enlightenment for the benefit of all beings. It is the heart of Mahayana Buddhism.
go_deeper_teaser: Bodhicitta: the most precious mind →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blessed perfection of wisdom.
context_note: Prajnaparamita — "perfection of wisdom" — is personified as a goddess and is the "mother of all buddhas." The Heart Sutra is her teaching.
go_deeper_teaser: Prajnaparamita: the mother of all buddhas →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blessed Akshobhya — the imperturbable.
context_note: Akshobhya — "imperturbable" — is the buddha of the east, representing mirror-like wisdom. His dark blue color represents the depth of the awakened mind.
go_deeper_teaser: The five dhyani buddhas: a complete map of awakening →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blessed Ratnasambhava — the jewel-born.
context_note: Ratnasambhava — "jewel-born" — is the buddha of the south, representing the wisdom of equality. All beings are equally precious jewels.
go_deeper_teaser: Ratnasambhava: the wisdom of equality →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blessed Amoghasiddhi — the infallible accomplisher.
context_note: Amoghasiddhi — "infallible accomplishment" — is the buddha of the north, representing all-accomplishing wisdom.
go_deeper_teaser: Amoghasiddhi: the all-accomplishing wisdom →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blessed Vairochana — the illuminator.
context_note: Vairochana — "the illuminator" — is the central buddha of the five dhyani buddhas, representing the dharmadhatu wisdom — the wisdom of all-encompassing space.
go_deeper_teaser: Vairochana: the central buddha of the mandala →

transliteration: Om Ah Ra Pa Ca Na Dhih Dhih Dhih
english_translation: Om — the wisdom syllables — wisdom, wisdom, wisdom.
phonetic_guide: OHM ah rah pah chah nah dhee dhee dhee
context_note: The extended Manjushri mantra with triple Dhih. Dhih is the seed syllable of wisdom — repeating it three times intensifies the awakening of prajna.
go_deeper_teaser: Prajna: the wisdom that liberates →

english_translation: Om — well-established — so be it.
context_note: Supratishthita — "well-established" — is a dharani for establishing the practice firmly. Svaha seals the intention.
go_deeper_teaser: Establishing a daily practice: the Buddhist approach →

english_translation: Om — earth-holder, earth-holder — so be it.
context_note: Dharani means both "earth" and "that which holds." This mantra grounds the practice in the earth of reality.
go_deeper_teaser: Dharani: the mantras that hold the earth →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blessed Vajrapani.
context_note: Vajrapani's thunderbolt cuts through the three poisons: greed, hatred, and delusion. This mantra invokes the power to overcome inner obstacles.
go_deeper_teaser: The three poisons: greed, hatred, and delusion →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blessed Kurukulle — the goddess of enchantment.
context_note: Kurukulle is a tantric goddess who enchants and magnetizes — drawing beings toward the Dharma through the power of love.
go_deeper_teaser: Kurukulle: the goddess who draws beings to the Dharma →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blessed Cunda — the goddess of awakening.
context_note: Cunda (or Chunda) is a bodhisattva associated with the awakening mind. She is depicted with multiple arms representing her many activities.
go_deeper_teaser: The female bodhisattvas: wisdom in feminine form →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the crusher of the vajra essence.
context_note: A powerful purification dharani. Pramardane — "crusher" — refers to the crushing of karmic obscurations.
go_deeper_teaser: Karma: what it really means and how to purify it →

english_translation: Om — purifier of all sins.
context_note: A dharani for purifying all negative karma. Vishoshana — "drying up" — suggests that negative karma is dried up like water in sunlight.
go_deeper_teaser: Purification practices in Buddhism →

english_translation: Om, I bow to all the blessed thus-gone ones.
context_note: A universal salutation to all buddhas of all times and directions. The awakened ones are infinite in number.
go_deeper_teaser: The infinite buddhas: the Buddhist vision of awakening →

english_translation: Om — the nature of the dharma realm.
context_note: Dharmadhatu — "dharma realm" — is the totality of all phenomena as they truly are. Svabhava — "own nature" — points to the intrinsic nature of mind.
go_deeper_teaser: Dharmadhatu: the realm of truth →

english_translation: Om — by nature pure are all phenomena.
context_note: The emptiness mantra — all phenomena are pure by nature. This is the Madhyamaka view: nothing is inherently defiled.
go_deeper_teaser: Emptiness: the most misunderstood Buddhist teaching →

english_translation: I am pure by nature.
context_note: The personal application of the emptiness mantra. Your true nature is pure — not because you have purified it, but because it was never defiled.
go_deeper_teaser: Buddha nature: the purity that was always there →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blessed thus-gone one.
context_note: A simple, universal invocation of the Buddha. Tathagata — "thus-gone" — points to the nature of awakening: going beyond all concepts.
go_deeper_teaser: Tathagata: the Buddha's most profound name →

english_translation: Om — become, become, greatly become.
context_note: A mantra of transformation. Bhava — "becoming" — is the process of continuous change. The mantra invites full participation in the process of becoming.
go_deeper_teaser: Impermanence: the Buddhist teaching on change →

english_translation: Om — complete awakening, complete awakening.
context_note: Sambodhi — "complete awakening" — is the full enlightenment of a buddha. Repeating it twice affirms the aspiration for complete awakening.
go_deeper_teaser: Sambodhi: what complete awakening actually means →

english_translation: Om — awakening, awakening, great awakening.
context_note: A mantra of progressive awakening. Bodhi — "awakening" — grows from individual to great, from partial to complete.
go_deeper_teaser: The stages of awakening in Buddhism →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blessed Avalokiteshvara — the one who hears all cries.
context_note: Avalokiteshvara — "the one who looks down with compassion" — is the bodhisattva who hears all cries of suffering and responds with compassion.
go_deeper_teaser: Avalokiteshvara: the bodhisattva of universal compassion →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the all-knowing one.
context_note: Sarvajnaya — "all-knowing" — is an epithet of the Buddha. Omniscience is not supernatural; it is the natural result of a completely purified mind.
go_deeper_teaser: Omniscience: the Buddhist understanding →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blessed Maitreya — the future Buddha of loving-kindness.
context_note: Maitreya — "the loving one" — is the future Buddha who will appear when the Dharma has been forgotten. His name is the root of the word "metta."
go_deeper_teaser: Maitreya: the future Buddha of loving-kindness →

english_translation: Om, I bow to the blessed Kshitigarbha — the earth treasury.
context_note: Kshitigarbha — "earth treasury" — is the bodhisattva who vowed to save all beings from the hell realms before becoming a buddha.
go_deeper_teaser: Kshitigarbha: the bodhisattva who descends to hell →

english_translation: Om — may it be accomplished — so be it.
context_note: A completion mantra. Sampadyante — "may it be accomplished" — seals any practice or intention with the wish for its fulfillment.
go_deeper_teaser: Sealing a practice: the importance of dedication →

english_translation: One God — True Name.
context_note: The Mool Mantar — the root mantra of Sikhism, composed by Guru Nanak. Ik Onkar — "One God" — is the foundational truth of Sikh theology.
go_deeper_teaser: The Mool Mantar: Sikhism's most sacred teaching →

english_translation: The Creator Being.
context_note: From the Mool Mantar. Karta — "creator" — Purakh — "being" or "person." The divine is not an abstract force but a personal creator.
go_deeper_teaser: Karta Purakh: the personal God of Sikhism →

english_translation: Without fear, without enmity.
context_note: From the Mool Mantar. The divine has no fear and no enemies. Meditating on these qualities transforms the practitioner.
go_deeper_teaser: Nirbhau: the fearless God of Guru Nanak →

english_translation: Timeless form — beyond time and death.
context_note: From the Mool Mantar. Akal — "beyond time" — Murat — "form." The divine is the timeless presence that underlies all temporal forms.
go_deeper_teaser: Akal: the timeless God of Sikhism →

english_translation: Unborn, self-illumined.
context_note: From the Mool Mantar. The divine was not born and does not need anything outside itself. Saibhang — "self-illumined" — the divine is its own light.
go_deeper_teaser: Saibhang: the self-illumined nature of God →

english_translation: By the grace of the Guru.
context_note: The final phrase of the Mool Mantar. All spiritual realization comes by the grace of the Guru — not by personal effort alone.
go_deeper_teaser: Gur Prasad: the grace that makes everything possible →

english_translation: Wondrous Guru — the divine that fills us with wonder.
context_note: The most important mantra in Sikhism. Wahe — "wondrous" — Guru — "teacher." The divine is the teacher who fills us with wonder.
go_deeper_teaser: Waheguru: the wonder at the heart of Sikhism →

english_translation: True Name — Wondrous Guru.
context_note: The most common Sikh mantra combination. Sat Nam — "True Name" — is the identity of the divine. Waheguru is the expression of wonder at that truth.
go_deeper_teaser: Sat Nam: the true name of God →

english_translation: True, great, and timeless.
context_note: The Sikh greeting and battle cry. Sri — "great" or "radiant" — Akal — "timeless." A reminder that truth is eternal.
go_deeper_teaser: Sat Sri Akal: the Sikh greeting and its meaning →

english_translation: Nanak, through the Name, may there be ever-rising spirit.
context_note: Chardi Kala — "ever-rising spirit" — is the Sikh concept of eternal optimism. No matter what happens, the spirit rises. This is the Sikh response to adversity.
go_deeper_teaser: Chardi Kala: the Sikh practice of eternal optimism →

english_translation: In your will, may all prosper.
context_note: The Ardas prayer's closing line. Sarbat — "all" — Da Bhala — "welfare." The Sikh prayer always ends with a wish for the welfare of all humanity.
go_deeper_teaser: Ardas: the Sikh prayer for all humanity →

english_translation: Remembrance, remembrance, remembrance.
context_note: Simran — "remembrance" or "meditation" — is the central Sikh practice. To remember God's name in every breath is the highest spiritual discipline.
go_deeper_teaser: Simran: the Sikh practice of divine remembrance →

english_translation: God, God, God, God — the creative infinity.
context_note: Har is the Sikh name for God as the creative force. Repeating it activates the navel center and the creative power of the divine.
go_deeper_teaser: Har: the creative name of God in Sikhism →

english_translation: Chant the name of God.
context_note: From the Guru Granth Sahib. Japahu — "chant" — is the imperative form. The Guru's instruction is direct: chant the name.
go_deeper_teaser: Japa: the practice of mantra repetition →

english_translation: Ram, Ram, Ram, Ram — the divine joy.
context_note: In Sikhism, Ram is not just the avatar but the divine name meaning "the one who permeates all." The Gurus used many divine names interchangeably.
go_deeper_teaser: Ram in Sikhism: the all-pervasive divine →

english_translation: Gobind, Gobind, Gobind — in the company of God.
context_note: From the Guru Granth Sahib. Gobind — "sustainer of the world" — is repeated three times, then "sang" — "in the company of." We are never alone.
go_deeper_teaser: Sangat: the Sikh community of truth →

english_translation: Chanting the Name, offer body and mind.
context_note: From the Guru Granth Sahib. The complete offering in Sikhism is not just words but the whole self — body and mind — surrendered to the divine name.
go_deeper_teaser: Total surrender: the Sikh path of devotion →

english_translation: With every breath, remember Gobind.
context_note: From the Guru Granth Sahib. Sas — "breath." The Sikh practice of remembering God with every breath transforms ordinary breathing into prayer.
go_deeper_teaser: Breath as prayer: the Sikh practice of Sas Sas →

english_translation: O mind, you are the image of divine light.
context_note: From the Guru Granth Sahib. Jot — "light" — Sarup — "form" or "image." The mind is not the enemy but the image of divine light.
go_deeper_teaser: Jot Sarup: the light within you →

english_translation: Recognize your own origin.
context_note: From the Guru Granth Sahib. Mul — "root" or "origin." The Guru's instruction: know where you come from. Your origin is divine.
go_deeper_teaser: Mul: returning to the root of your being →

english_translation: As is your will, so be it.
context_note: From the Guru Granth Sahib. Raza — "will" or "pleasure." Complete acceptance of the divine will is the highest Sikh spiritual state.
go_deeper_teaser: Raza: the Sikh practice of divine will acceptance →

english_translation: Nanak is the servant at your door.
context_note: Guru Nanak's signature of humility. Das — "servant." The greatest teacher called himself a servant at the divine door.
go_deeper_teaser: Guru Nanak: the servant who became the teacher →

english_translation: Without you, there is no other.
context_note: From the Guru Granth Sahib. A declaration of non-duality: there is only God. Everything else is the play of the one divine reality.
go_deeper_teaser: Sikh non-dualism: there is only one →

english_translation: Fully pervading all with all powers.
context_note: From the Guru Granth Sahib. Sarab — "all" — Kala — "powers" — Bharpoor — "fully pervading." The divine is fully present everywhere with all its powers.
go_deeper_teaser: The omnipresent God of the Guru Granth Sahib →

english_translation: The primordial sound — the vibration from which all creation arises.
context_note: Om is the first sound — the vibration of pure consciousness before it becomes words or thoughts. It contains all sounds within it.
go_deeper_teaser: The physics of Om: why it is the primordial sound →

english_translation: Three Oms — for body, speech, and mind.
context_note: Three Oms purify the three aspects of our being: the physical body, speech and breath, and the mind. A complete practice in three sounds.
go_deeper_teaser: The three Oms: a complete purification practice →

english_translation: Om — peace, peace, peace.
context_note: The universal peace invocation ending all Vedic prayers. Three repetitions address the three sources of suffering: adhyatmika (self), adhibhautika (others), adhidaivika (cosmos).
go_deeper_teaser: The three sources of suffering and their antidote →

english_translation: I am that — the breath itself is the mantra.
phonetic_guide: SOH-hahm (So on inhale, Ham on exhale)
context_note: So'ham is the natural sound of the breath: So on the inhale, Ham on the exhale. The breath itself is saying "I am that" 21,600 times per day.
go_deeper_teaser: So'ham: the mantra you've been chanting since birth →

english_translation: The swan — the breath — I am that.
phonetic_guide: HAHM-sah (Ham on exhale, Sa on inhale)
context_note: Hamsa — "swan" or "I am that" — is So'ham reversed. The swan is the symbol of the discriminating mind that separates milk from water.
go_deeper_teaser: Hamsa: the swan of discernment →

english_translation: I am Brahman — I am the infinite.
context_note: One of the four mahavakyas — great sayings — of the Upanishads. From the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad. The direct declaration of identity with the infinite.
go_deeper_teaser: The four mahavakyas: the Upanishads' greatest declarations →

english_translation: That thou art — you are that infinite reality.
context_note: The second mahavakya from the Chandogya Upanishad. The teacher's declaration to the student: you are not separate from the infinite.
